content:
Kernel methods grew from **functional analysis** and **reproducing kernel Hilbert spaces (RKHS)** — the mathematical setting where inner products ⟨φ(x), φ(x′)⟩ are well-defined even when φ is infinite-dimensional.

**Aizerman, Braverman, and Rozonoer (1964)** showed that pattern-recognition algorithms depending only on dot products could be generalized by replacing those products with arbitrary positive kernels — the conceptual seed of the **kernel trick**.

**Boser, Guyon, and Vapnik (1992)** applied kernels to **support vector machines**, turning a linear maximum-margin classifier into a nonlinear one without explicit feature maps.

**Vapnik (1995)** and successors formalized **Mercer's theorem** as the practical test for valid kernels.

**Schölkopf, Smola, and colleagues (1990s–2000s)** extended kernels to **kernel PCA**, **Gaussian processes**, and a zoo of domain-specific string kernels.

The **deep learning** resurgence shifted emphasis toward **learned φ** via neural networks, but hand-designed kernels remain strong on **small tabular** problems and as theoretical glue linking similarity, geometry, and linear separators.

timeline.label: RKHS foundations (1900s–1950s)
timeline.description: Mercer and Hilbert-space theory establish when a similarity function corresponds to an inner product.

timeline.label: Generalized dot-product methods (1964)
timeline.description: Aizerman et al. show algorithms using only inner products can be kernelized.

timeline.label: Kernel SVM (1992)
timeline.description: Boser, Guyon, Vapnik introduce nonlinear SVMs via polynomial and RBF kernels.

timeline.label: Kernel methods textbook era (2000s)
timeline.description: Kernel PCA, GPs, and SVM toolkits (LIBSVM, scikit-learn) popularize K in industry.

timeline.label: Learned representations (2010s–present)
timeline.description: Deep nets learn φ end-to-end; kernels remain canonical for theory and compact tabular models.
